text: The surgeon dissects the hiatus and the angle of His, clearly exposing these anatomical structures during the robot-assisted gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon dissects the esophagus away from the right and left crura, encircling the esophagus to achieve adequate length for closure of the hiatus.
The surgeon uses zero Ethibond pledgeted sutures to complete the hiatal repair, placing three sutures to secure the repair.
The surgeon employs a perigastric approach to create a small gastric pouch approximately five centimeters from the gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon creates a small gastric pouch after defining the retrogastric plane, using surgical instruments to carefully manipulate and shape the stomach tissue.
The surgeon uses a stapler with reinforcement to create the small gastric pouch, completing the formation of the pouch at the specified location near the gastroesophageal junction.
A 34 French Ewald tube is inserted to calibrate the gastric pouch. The surgeon then uses a stapler to make vertical fires, completing the creation of a small gastric pouch based on the lesser curve.
The surgeon completes the creation of the gastric pouch and proceeds to divide the greater omentum, separating it from the stomach using surgical instruments.
The surgeon prepares the surgical field for the gastroenterostomy, adjusting the setup based on the specific requirements of the case.
The surgeon performs an omental division to facilitate the gastroenterogenostomy, ensuring minimal tension in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz by reflecting the transverse mesocolon, then constructs a 50-centimeter biliary limb.
The surgeon performs a gastrotomy and a jejunotomy, creating openings in the stomach and jejunum to facilitate the gastroenterostomy.
The surgeon uses a blue load stapler to create the gastrojejunal anastomosis, joining the stomach to the jejunum.

The Ewald tube is advanced into position, and a two OV absorbable V-lock suture is used to close the common enterotomy. The closure is performed in two layers by running the V-lock suture backwards to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon converts the loop gastrojejunostomy to a Roux configuration by dividing the afferent limb with a white load robotic stapler, creating a biliopancreatic limb on the patient's left side and a Roux limb on the right side.

The surgeon measures approximately one hundred centimeters distal on the Roux limb and performs a side-to-side anastomosis using the H-shaped technique. The stapler is fired bidirectionally to form the H-shaped configuration.
The surgeon performs the first stapler fire in one direction to begin the H-shaped anastomosis. The common enterotomy is then closed transversely using a stapler, completing the anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the mesentery defect using a permanent 2-0 Ethibond suture, carefully avoiding the creation of a mesenteric hematoma.

The surgeon exposes Peterson's defect by reflecting the Roux limb to the patient's left side and lifting the transverse mesocolon. The defect is then closed by suturing it to the edge of the transverse colon.
The surgeon performs an endoscopy to visually inspect the newly created anastomosis, ensuring proper alignment and integrity of the surgical connection.
